_meta.description: Legislative / regulatory context for each U.S. state's PFAS response. Keyed by two-letter state code. Can be regenerated by scripts/generate-state-content.ts.
_meta.source: Public records, state agency publications, EPA UCMR 5 data.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Alabama has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Alabama Department of Environmental Management has participated in federal UCMR 5 sampling coordination but has not issued enforceable state MCLs of its own.

State records show ongoing litigation connected to 3M and other chemical manufacturers operating in the Decatur area, where historic PFAS releases into the Tennessee River have driven multiple settlements with downstream utilities. Water systems in Alabama remain subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
AL.attribution: Based on Alabama Department of Environmental Management publications and public litigation records.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Alaska has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation maintains action levels for site investigations at legacy firefighting foam release locations, including civilian airports and former military installations such as the Fairbanks area, but these are cleanup triggers rather than enforceable drinking water limits.

State legislators have introduced several PFAS bills in recent sessions covering foam takeback and testing at state airports. Public water systems are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
AK.attribution: Based on Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation PFAS action level guidance.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Arizona has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Arizona Department of Environmental Quality has been tracking contamination near former military airfields in the Tucson and Luke Air Force Base areas, where AFFF firefighting foam releases have affected groundwater.

State records show the Tucson Airport Remediation Project has expanded to address PFAS alongside legacy solvent plumes. Water systems in Arizona remain on the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
AZ.attribution: Based on Arizona Department of Environmental Quality publications.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Arkansas has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Arkansas Department of Energy and Environment has not published enforceable state MCLs and generally defers to federal action on drinking water regulation.
Public water systems in Arkansas are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
AR.attribution: Based on Arkansas Department of Energy and Environment publications.

paragraphs: California has been one of the most active states on PFAS, although it has not set enforceable drinking water MCLs at the state level. The California State Water Resources Control Board, through its Division of Drinking Water, has published notification levels and response levels that require utilities to act when certain thresholds are met. The notification levels include 5.1 parts per trillion for PFOA and 6.5 parts per trillion for PFOS, both stricter than the federal MCLs of 4 parts per trillion finalized in April 2024 but advisory rather than enforceable.

The California legislature has passed several product-level PFAS laws. AB 1200, signed in 2021, banned intentionally added PFAS in food packaging beginning in 2023. AB 652 restricted PFAS in products intended for children. AB 1817 banned PFAS in textiles starting in 2025. SB 903, introduced in 2024, proposed eliminating PFAS in most consumer products by 2030, and elements of it have advanced through committee.

State records show the Division of Drinking Water has required hundreds of California utilities to sample for PFAS beyond the federal UCMR 5 list, and the state has published source water sampling results on an interactive public dashboard. Several California cities have shut down contaminated wells and brought treatment online following detections.

Litigation has been active, with the California Attorney General joining multistate actions against 3M, DuPont, and other manufacturers. Separately, the state has investigated PFAS contamination from landfill leachate in the Chiquita Canyon area north of Los Angeles. Water systems in California are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029, in addition to the state notification level framework.
CA.attribution: Based on California State Water Resources Control Board publications and California legislative records.

paragraphs: Colorado has enacted some of the more active PFAS legislation in the Mountain West. HB21-1189, signed in 2021, prohibits the use of fluorinated firefighting foam at oil and gas facilities and restricted AFFF use at state training sites. HB22-1345, signed in 2022, banned the sale of PFAS in several product categories including carpets, fabric treatments, cosmetics, and food packaging, with staggered effective dates running through 2027.

State records show the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment has maintained a PFAS Action Plan and operates a sampling program focused on public water systems near known contamination sources, including Peterson Space Force Base and the Suncor refinery area north of Denver. The Water Quality Control Division has used Policy 20-1 as a state-level guidance value for site investigation cleanups, though it is not an enforceable drinking water MCL.

Litigation has been ongoing, with the Colorado Attorney General joining multistate actions against 3M and DuPont. Public water systems are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
CO.attribution: Based on Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment publications and Colorado legislative records.

paragraphs: Connecticut has taken an active role on PFAS since 2019, when the governor's interagency PFAS Task Force released the Connecticut PFAS Action Plan. The plan set up an AFFF take-back program that collected tens of thousands of gallons of fluorinated firefighting foam from municipal fire departments for safe disposal, one of the larger state-run collection efforts in the country.

The Connecticut Department of Energy and Environmental Protection has not adopted enforceable state MCLs for PFAS in drinking water, relying instead on the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The state Department of Public Health has issued drinking water advisories near contaminated sites and required remedial actions, including at the former Loomis Chaffee School area in Windsor and the Kellogg-Deering well field in Norwalk.

State legislators passed SB 837 in 2021 banning PFAS in food packaging and firefighting foam, with product exemptions phased out through 2023. Water systems in Connecticut are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
CT.attribution: Based on Connecticut PFAS Action Plan and Connecticut Department of Energy and Environmental Protection publications.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Delaware has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Delaware Department of Natural Resources and Environmental Control has tracked contamination near the Dover Air Force Base and New Castle County Airport, where AFFF firefighting foam releases have affected groundwater, and has required monitoring of nearby private wells.

State records show Delaware has joined multistate litigation against 3M and DuPont. Public water systems are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
DE.attribution: Based on Delaware Department of Natural Resources and Environmental Control publications.

paragraphs: The District of Columbia does not set its own drinking water MCLs for PFAS. DC Water and the DC Department of Energy and Environment rely on the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024, which set enforceable limits of 4 parts per trillion for PFOA and PFOS and 10 parts per trillion for PFHxS, PFNA, and HFPO-DA.

Public records show DC Water sampled for PFAS under UCMR 5 and has reported results through annual Consumer Confidence Reports. The district is subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
DC.attribution: Based on DC Water and DC Department of Energy and Environment publications.

paragraphs: Florida has not adopted enforceable state-level PFAS drinking water MCLs beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Florida Department of Environmental Protection maintains provisional cleanup target levels for soil and groundwater at contaminated sites, and has focused enforcement attention on former military airfields including Naval Air Station Jacksonville and Whiting Field.

State records show Stuart, Florida is the lead plaintiff in the federal 3M AFFF multidistrict litigation based in Charleston, South Carolina, and the first bellwether settlement payments have begun flowing to affected water utilities. The city has rebuilt its treatment system using granular activated carbon.
Public water systems in Florida are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
FL.attribution: Based on Florida Department of Environmental Protection publications and federal 3M AFFF MDL court records.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Georgia has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Georgia Environmental Protection Division has been tracking PFAS contamination tied to the carpet manufacturing industry concentrated around Dalton in the state's northwest corner, where Rome, Calhoun, and other downstream Coosa River basin utilities have reported detections above federal limits.

State records show multiple Georgia cities have joined litigation against 3M, DuPont, and carpet manufacturers, and several settlements have been reached to fund new treatment plants. A PBS FRONTLINE documentary on the Dalton-area contamination aired in early 2026, increasing public pressure on state lawmakers.
Public water systems in Georgia are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
GA.attribution: Based on Georgia Environmental Protection Division publications and federal court records.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Hawaii has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Hawaii Department of Health has been engaged with the Navy on PFAS contamination near the Red Hill fuel facility on Oahu and at former military airfields on several islands, where AFFF firefighting foam releases have affected groundwater.
Public water systems in Hawaii are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
HI.attribution: Based on Hawaii Department of Health publications.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Idaho has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Idaho Department of Environmental Quality has tracked contamination near Mountain Home Air Force Base and has not published enforceable state MCLs.
Public water systems in Idaho are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
ID.attribution: Based on Idaho Department of Environmental Quality publications.

paragraphs: Illinois has adopted health advisory guidance levels for six PFAS compounds through the Illinois Environmental Protection Agency, including a PFOA guidance level of 2 parts per trillion and a PFOS guidance level of 14 parts per trillion, both advisory rather than enforceable. The state has conducted a statewide PFAS sampling investigation of community water supplies under an EPA grant.

The Illinois legislature passed the PFAS Reduction Act in 2021, prohibiting the sale and use of Class B firefighting foam containing intentionally added PFAS. State records show Illinois has joined multistate litigation against 3M and DuPont, and several utilities near former industrial and military sites have reported exceedances.
Public water systems in Illinois are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
IL.attribution: Based on Illinois Environmental Protection Agency publications and Illinois legislative records.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Indiana has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Indiana Department of Environmental Management has coordinated with federal partners on UCMR 5 sampling and has not published enforceable state MCLs.
Public water systems in Indiana are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
IN.attribution: Based on Indiana Department of Environmental Management publications.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Iowa has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Iowa Department of Natural Resources has conducted a voluntary PFAS sampling initiative at public water supplies, with results posted publicly. Detections at municipal supplies in central Iowa prompted expanded sampling during 2023 and 2024.
Public water systems in Iowa are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
IA.attribution: Based on Iowa Department of Natural Resources publications.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Kansas has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Kansas Department of Health and Environment has tracked contamination near McConnell Air Force Base and has not published enforceable state MCLs.
Public water systems in Kansas are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
KS.attribution: Based on Kansas Department of Health and Environment publications.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Kentucky has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Kentucky Division of Water has monitored contamination in Ohio River basin communities downstream of the DuPont Washington Works plant in West Virginia, which is the historical source of much PFOA contamination in the region.
Public water systems in Kentucky are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
KY.attribution: Based on Kentucky Division of Water publications.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Louisiana has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Louisiana Department of Environmental Quality has coordinated federal UCMR 5 sampling and has not published enforceable state MCLs.
Public water systems in Louisiana are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
LA.attribution: Based on Louisiana Department of Environmental Quality publications.

paragraphs: Maine has enacted some of the strictest PFAS laws in the country. LD 1503, signed by Governor Janet Mills in 2021, requires reporting of intentionally added PFAS in products sold in Maine and bans the sale of most consumer products containing intentionally added PFAS by 2030. The law was amended in 2023 to stagger effective dates and add exemptions for specific uses, but the core product ban remains.

Maine's drinking water response began after the Maine Department of Environmental Protection discovered widespread farm contamination from biosolids land-applied as fertilizer, most famously on the Stoneridge Farm in Arundel and farms in Fairfield. The state created an interim PFAS drinking water standard of 20 parts per trillion for the combined total of six PFAS compounds, including PFOA, PFOS, PFHxS, PFHpA, PFNA, and PFDA.

The Maine legislature appropriated tens of millions of dollars for farm remediation, blood testing programs, and impacted household compensation through the PFAS Fund. State records show Maine has required all public water systems to sample for PFAS under the state standard, in addition to federal UCMR 5 requirements.

Litigation has been ongoing. The Maine Attorney General has joined multistate actions against 3M and DuPont, and Maine farmers have pursued separate claims against biosolids handlers. Public water systems are subject to both the Maine 20 parts per trillion combined standard and the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
ME.attribution: Based on Maine Department of Environmental Protection publications and Maine legislative records.

paragraphs: Maryland has adopted several PFAS-related laws. HB 275 in 2020 banned the sale and use of Class B firefighting foam containing PFAS, with exemptions for certain federally required uses. HB 0373 in 2022 banned PFAS in food packaging and rugs.

The Maryland Department of the Environment has not adopted enforceable drinking water MCLs of its own and relies on the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. State records show MDE has monitored contamination near Joint Base Andrews and the St. Inigoes Naval Air Station in the southern part of the state, and the state has joined multistate litigation.
Public water systems in Maryland are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
MD.attribution: Based on Maryland Department of the Environment publications and Maryland legislative records.

paragraphs: Massachusetts was one of the first states to adopt an enforceable drinking water MCL for PFAS. In 2020 the Massachusetts Department of Environmental Protection finalized a state MCL of 20 parts per trillion for the sum of six PFAS compounds, including PFOA, PFOS, PFHxS, PFNA, PFHpA, and PFDA. This is a stricter combined threshold than the federal EPA individual MCLs finalized in April 2024, though the federal rule regulates each compound separately.

The MassDEP required all public water systems in the state to begin sampling for these six compounds on a defined schedule, and utilities that exceeded the 20 parts per trillion threshold were required to notify customers and begin treatment or alternate supply planning. The state has published ongoing results publicly.

The Massachusetts legislature has appropriated funding for PFAS treatment grants through the state Clean Water Trust. State records show MassDEP has focused enforcement attention on contamination near Joint Base Cape Cod and the former Westover Air Reserve Base, both of which have extensive AFFF firefighting foam plumes.
Public water systems in Massachusetts are subject to both the state PFAS6 standard and the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
MA.attribution: Based on Massachusetts Department of Environmental Protection publications and Massachusetts legislative records.

paragraphs: Michigan has one of the most developed state-level PFAS programs in the country. Governor Rick Snyder created the Michigan PFAS Action Response Team in 2017, and Governor Gretchen Whitmer continued and expanded the program. MPART coordinates PFAS work across the state environmental, health, agricultural, and military affairs departments.

In 2020 Michigan adopted enforceable drinking water MCLs for seven PFAS compounds through the Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy. The Michigan MCLs are 8 parts per trillion for PFOA, 16 parts per trillion for PFOS, 51 parts per trillion for PFHxS, 6 parts per trillion for PFNA, 420 parts per trillion for PFBS, 370 parts per trillion for HFPO-DA, and 400,000 parts per trillion for PFHxA. The PFOA and PFNA limits are stricter than the federal EPA MCLs finalized in April 2024, while the PFOS limit is weaker than the federal 4 parts per trillion.

State records show Michigan has required every community water supply in the state to sample for PFAS under the MCL rule, and results are posted to the Michigan PFAS dashboard. Communities that have exceeded limits include Parchment, where PFOS was detected at extreme levels in 2018, Oscoda near the former Wurtsmith Air Force Base, and Rockford downstream of the Wolverine Worldwide tannery.

The Michigan Attorney General has filed lawsuits against 3M, DuPont, Chemours, and other manufacturers seeking funding for cleanup and treatment. State records show Michigan has appropriated hundreds of millions of dollars for PFAS response through annual budgets. Public water systems are subject to both the Michigan MCLs and the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
MI.attribution: Based on Michigan PFAS Action Response Team publications, Michigan Department of Environment Great Lakes and Energy records, and Michigan Attorney General filings.

paragraphs: Minnesota was at the center of one of the largest PFAS settlements in U.S. history. In 2018 the state settled a lawsuit against 3M for 850 million dollars to address contamination of drinking water supplies in the East Metro area of the Twin Cities, where 3M had manufactured and disposed of PFAS for decades. Funds from the settlement have paid for treatment plants, alternate water supplies, and ongoing monitoring in affected communities.

The Minnesota legislature passed HF 2310 in 2023, known as Amara's Law after Amara Strande, a young woman who died of a rare cancer and grew up in the affected East Metro area. The law bans the sale of products containing intentionally added PFAS in several categories starting in 2025, with broader bans through 2032. Minnesota is one of the few states matching Maine's aggressive product-level approach.

The Minnesota Department of Health has published health-based values for several PFAS compounds that are used as drinking water guidance values, including a PFOA value of 35 parts per trillion based on older toxicology, now superseded for enforcement purposes by the federal EPA MCLs. The Minnesota Pollution Control Agency continues to monitor groundwater contamination at the 3M Cottage Grove, Oakdale, and Woodbury disposal sites.

Public water systems in Minnesota are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029, in addition to ongoing state monitoring requirements under the 3M consent order.
MN.attribution: Based on Minnesota Department of Health publications, Minnesota Pollution Control Agency records, and Minnesota legislative records.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Mississippi has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Mississippi Department of Environmental Quality has not published enforceable state MCLs.
Public water systems in Mississippi are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
MS.attribution: Based on Mississippi Department of Environmental Quality publications.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Missouri has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Missouri Department of Natural Resources has conducted PFAS sampling at public water supplies in coordination with the federal UCMR 5 program.
Public water systems in Missouri are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
MO.attribution: Based on Missouri Department of Natural Resources publications.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Montana has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Montana Department of Environmental Quality has tracked contamination near Malmstrom Air Force Base outside Great Falls, where AFFF firefighting foam releases have affected groundwater.
Public water systems in Montana are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
MT.attribution: Based on Montana Department of Environmental Quality publications.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Nebraska has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Nebraska Department of Environment and Energy has monitored contamination near Offutt Air Force Base south of Omaha.
Public water systems in Nebraska are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
NE.attribution: Based on Nebraska Department of Environment and Energy publications.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Nevada has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Nevada Division of Environmental Protection has tracked contamination near Nellis Air Force Base outside Las Vegas and the former Fallon Naval Air Station.
Public water systems in Nevada are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
NV.attribution: Based on Nevada Division of Environmental Protection publications.

paragraphs: New Hampshire was among the earliest states to adopt enforceable PFAS drinking water standards. In 2019 the New Hampshire Department of Environmental Services finalized MCLs of 12 parts per trillion for PFOA, 15 parts per trillion for PFOS, 18 parts per trillion for PFHxS, and 11 parts per trillion for PFNA. The PFOA, PFHxS, and PFNA values are stricter than the federal EPA MCLs finalized in April 2024, while the PFOS value is weaker than the federal 4 parts per trillion.

The impetus for state action came from contamination in the Merrimack area linked to the Saint-Gobain Performance Plastics facility. Residents led by Laurene Allen pushed successfully for state legislation and a remediation agreement with Saint-Gobain that has funded alternate water supplies for hundreds of homes. Saint-Gobain signed a major remediation deal with the state in early 2026.

The New Hampshire legislature has appropriated funding for PFAS response and testing. State records show NHDES has required all community water systems to sample for PFAS under the state MCL rule and has maintained a publicly accessible results dashboard. The state has also pursued litigation against 3M and DuPont that resulted in settlement payments flowing into a remediation fund.
Public water systems in New Hampshire are subject to both the state MCLs and the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
NH.attribution: Based on New Hampshire Department of Environmental Services publications and New Hampshire legislative records.

paragraphs: New Jersey was the first state in the country to set an enforceable drinking water MCL for a PFAS compound. In 2018 the New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection adopted an MCL of 13 parts per trillion for PFNA, and in 2020 followed with MCLs of 14 parts per trillion for PFOA and 13 parts per trillion for PFOS. These state values are stricter than the federal EPA MCLs of 4 parts per trillion for PFOA and PFOS finalized in April 2024 for the PFOA comparison but less strict for PFOS, while the PFNA value is stricter than the federal 10 parts per trillion.

The NJDEP issued Directive Number 1 in 2019 naming DuPont, Chemours, 3M, Solvay, and Arkema as responsible parties and ordering them to fund investigation and cleanup of statewide PFAS contamination. State records show the directive has driven tens of millions of dollars in remediation funding. Major contaminated sites include the DuPont Chambers Works in Pennsville, the Solvay plant in West Deptford, and the Paulsboro area.

The New Jersey legislature has passed product-level laws including a ban on PFAS in firefighting foam, in food packaging, and in cosmetics. The state has also published a PFAS Treatment and Destruction Roadmap through NJDEP.

Litigation has been extensive. The New Jersey Attorney General has filed lawsuits against 3M, DuPont, Chemours, Solvay, and others that have produced settlements valued in the hundreds of millions of dollars. Public water systems in New Jersey are subject to both the state MCLs and the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
NJ.attribution: Based on New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection publications and New Jersey Attorney General filings.

paragraphs: New Mexico has been active on PFAS enforcement at former military airfields. The New Mexico Environment Department filed suit against the U.S. Air Force over PFAS plumes at Cannon Air Force Base near Clovis and Holloman Air Force Base near Alamogordo, arguing the federal government must fund cleanup and alternate water supplies for affected dairy farms and communities.

State records show New Mexico has not adopted enforceable state drinking water MCLs for PFAS and relies on the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The legislature passed HB 212 in 2022 banning PFAS in firefighting foam used for training exercises.
Public water systems in New Mexico are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
NM.attribution: Based on New Mexico Environment Department publications and federal court records.

paragraphs: New York adopted enforceable drinking water MCLs for PFAS in 2020. The New York State Department of Health set MCLs of 10 parts per trillion for PFOA and 10 parts per trillion for PFOS, along with a 1 part per billion MCL for 1,4-dioxane. The state PFOA and PFOS limits are less strict than the federal EPA MCLs of 4 parts per trillion finalized in April 2024, and New York utilities now must meet the stricter federal standards.

The impetus for state action came from contamination in Hoosick Falls, where Michael Hickey's investigation of his father's cancer death exposed widespread PFOA pollution from the Saint-Gobain and Honeywell plastics plants starting in 2014. That case broke PFAS into national news and drove New York's regulatory response. Other major contaminated communities include Newburgh, where the Stewart Air National Guard Base contaminated the city water supply, and Petersburgh in the eastern part of the state.

The New York legislature has passed several product-level laws. A 2022 law banned the sale of apparel and outdoor apparel containing intentionally added PFAS, with the main provisions taking effect in 2025. Other bans have covered PFAS in food packaging and carpets. The state Attorney General has filed lawsuits against 3M, DuPont, and other manufacturers that have produced significant settlements.

State records show the Department of Environmental Conservation maintains a PFAS sampling and cleanup program at state Superfund sites and has published an interactive contamination map. Public water systems in New York are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
NY.attribution: Based on New York State Department of Health publications, New York State Department of Environmental Conservation records, and New York State legislative records.

paragraphs: North Carolina is the home of the GenX contamination case, one of the defining PFAS investigations of the last decade. In 2017 researchers and the Wilmington StarNews reported that the Chemours Fayetteville Works facility had been releasing the PFAS replacement compound HFPO-DA, sold as GenX, into the Cape Fear River for years, affecting drinking water for hundreds of thousands of residents downstream in Wilmington and surrounding communities.

The North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality issued a consent order against Chemours in 2019 requiring the company to reduce emissions, provide filtration systems for nearby private wells, and fund long-term monitoring. The state has published a health advisory of 140 parts per trillion for GenX, which was in place before the federal EPA MCL of 10 parts per trillion for HFPO-DA finalized in April 2024.

State records show NCDEQ has identified dozens of contaminated private wells around the Chemours plant and has required the company to install reverse osmosis systems for affected households. A consent order amendment in 2022 expanded the area covered. The North Carolina legislature has appropriated funding for PFAS research at the NC PFAS Testing Network, a consortium of state universities.

Litigation has been active. The Cape Fear Public Utility Authority has sued Chemours and DuPont seeking funding for treatment plant upgrades, and the state Attorney General has joined multistate actions. Public water systems in North Carolina are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
NC.attribution: Based on North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality publications, NC PFAS Testing Network research, and federal court records.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, North Dakota has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The North Dakota Department of Environmental Quality has tracked contamination near Grand Forks Air Force Base and Minot Air Force Base.
Public water systems in North Dakota are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
ND.attribution: Based on North Dakota Department of Environmental Quality publications.

paragraphs: Ohio has a long history with PFAS that predates most other state responses. The DuPont Washington Works plant across the river in Parkersburg, West Virginia released PFOA into the Ohio River for decades, contaminating drinking water in southeastern Ohio communities including Belpre, Little Hocking, and Tuppers Plains. The 2017 C8 Science Panel settlement with DuPont funded health studies and medical monitoring for tens of thousands of affected residents on both sides of the river.

The Ohio Environmental Protection Agency launched a statewide PFAS Action Plan in 2019 and began sampling public water systems across the state. Ohio EPA has published results publicly. State records show the agency has adopted state-level action levels for PFAS in drinking water that were in place before the federal EPA MCLs finalized in April 2024, though these Ohio values are advisory rather than enforceable MCLs.

The Ohio Attorney General has filed lawsuits against 3M, DuPont, and Chemours seeking funding for statewide cleanup. Litigation has also targeted firefighting foam manufacturers. State records show several Ohio utilities have reported exceedances above the federal MCLs and have begun installing treatment.
Public water systems in Ohio are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
OH.attribution: Based on Ohio Environmental Protection Agency publications, Ohio Attorney General filings, and the C8 Health Project records.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Oklahoma has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Oklahoma Department of Environmental Quality has tracked contamination near Tinker Air Force Base in Midwest City and Vance Air Force Base near Enid.
Public water systems in Oklahoma are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
OK.attribution: Based on Oklahoma Department of Environmental Quality publications.

paragraphs: Oregon has taken steps on PFAS but has not adopted enforceable state drinking water MCLs. The Oregon Department of Environmental Quality published a PFAS Strategic Plan in 2020 and has coordinated sampling across community water supplies. The Oregon Health Authority maintains drinking water guidance values for several PFAS compounds that match or are stricter than older EPA health advisories.

State records show contamination has been identified near Portland Air National Guard Base and several former industrial sites in the Portland metro area. The Oregon legislature has passed bans on PFAS in food packaging and in firefighting foam. Public water systems are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
OR.attribution: Based on Oregon Department of Environmental Quality and Oregon Health Authority publications.

paragraphs: Pennsylvania adopted enforceable drinking water MCLs for PFOA and PFOS in 2023 through the Department of Environmental Protection. The Pennsylvania MCLs are 14 parts per trillion for PFOA and 18 parts per trillion for PFOS, both less strict than the federal EPA values of 4 parts per trillion finalized in April 2024. Pennsylvania utilities now must meet the stricter federal standards.

The impetus for Pennsylvania's regulatory response came from extreme contamination in Bucks and Montgomery counties around the former Naval Air Warfare Center Warminster, Naval Air Station Joint Reserve Base Willow Grove, and Horsham Air Guard Station. AFFF firefighting foam releases at these sites contaminated public and private drinking water for tens of thousands of residents, including one measurement of combined PFOS and PFOA at over 22,000 parts per trillion in off-base wells.

The Pennsylvania legislature created a PFAS Action Team, and state records show PADEP has required sampling at all community water systems. The Pennsylvania Attorney General has filed lawsuits against 3M, DuPont, and other manufacturers. The BuxMont PFAS Coalition has advocated for ongoing remediation funding.
Public water systems in Pennsylvania are subject to both the state MCLs and the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
PA.attribution: Based on Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection publications and Pennsylvania Attorney General filings.

paragraphs: Rhode Island adopted an enforceable state drinking water standard for PFAS in 2022. The Rhode Island Department of Health set an MCL of 20 parts per trillion for the combined total of six PFAS compounds, including PFOA, PFOS, PFHxS, PFHpA, PFNA, and PFDA. This combined approach is similar to the Massachusetts PFAS6 standard and is stricter than the federal EPA individual MCLs for the lower-level compounds but less strict for PFOA and PFOS individually.

The Rhode Island legislature also passed product-level laws banning PFAS in food packaging and Class B firefighting foam. State records show RIDOH has required all community water systems to sample under the state standard, and results are publicly posted. Public water systems in Rhode Island are subject to both the state combined MCL and the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
RI.attribution: Based on Rhode Island Department of Health publications and Rhode Island legislative records.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, South Carolina has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The South Carolina Department of Environmental Services, which replaced the former DHEC environmental programs, has coordinated federal UCMR 5 sampling. The state is also the venue for the federal 3M AFFF multidistrict litigation based in the U.S. District Court for the District of South Carolina in Charleston.
Public water systems in South Carolina are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
SC.attribution: Based on South Carolina Department of Environmental Services publications and federal court records.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, South Dakota has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The South Dakota Department of Agriculture and Natural Resources has tracked contamination near Ellsworth Air Force Base outside Rapid City.
Public water systems in South Dakota are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
SD.attribution: Based on South Dakota Department of Agriculture and Natural Resources publications.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Tennessee has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Tennessee Department of Environment and Conservation has coordinated federal UCMR 5 sampling and has investigated contamination at several industrial sites in the Chattanooga area.
Public water systems in Tennessee are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
TN.attribution: Based on Tennessee Department of Environment and Conservation publications.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Texas has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Texas Commission on Environmental Quality has coordinated federal UCMR 5 sampling. State records show contamination has been identified near several former and active military airfields in Texas, including Joint Base San Antonio and the former Naval Air Station Fort Worth Joint Reserve Base.

The Texas Attorney General has joined some multistate PFAS-related litigation. Public water systems in Texas are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
TX.attribution: Based on Texas Commission on Environmental Quality publications and federal court records.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Utah has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Utah Department of Environmental Quality has tracked contamination near Hill Air Force Base, where the Air Force has been conducting PFAS investigation and remediation work for several years.
Public water systems in Utah are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
UT.attribution: Based on Utah Department of Environmental Quality publications.

paragraphs: Vermont has been a national leader on PFAS policy. The state response began with the 2016 discovery of extensive PFOA contamination in Bennington and North Bennington from the former ChemFab facility and its successor Saint-Gobain Performance Plastics. Testing revealed PFOA levels in some private wells as high as 2,880 parts per trillion and a plume that continues to spread. Saint-Gobain signed a settlement agreement with the state to extend municipal water to hundreds of affected households.

In 2019 the Vermont legislature passed Act 21, directing the Agency of Natural Resources to adopt an enforceable drinking water MCL for PFAS. The resulting rule set a combined MCL of 20 parts per trillion for five PFAS compounds, including PFOA, PFOS, PFHxS, PFHpA, and PFNA. Vermont was among the first states to regulate multiple PFAS as a group with a combined threshold.

The Vermont legislature has passed additional product-level laws including S.20, which bans PFAS in food packaging, carpets, fabric treatments, ski wax, and menstrual products, with staggered effective dates. Firefighter protection laws have also restricted Class B foam. The state has an active PFAS advisory committee that makes policy recommendations to lawmakers.

Litigation has been ongoing. The Vermont Attorney General has joined multistate actions against 3M and DuPont, and Vermont residents have pursued separate claims. Public water systems in Vermont are subject to both the state combined MCL and the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
VT.attribution: Based on Vermont Agency of Natural Resources publications and Vermont legislative records.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Virginia has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Virginia Department of Environmental Quality has coordinated federal UCMR 5 sampling and has tracked contamination near Langley Air Force Base in Hampton and several other former and active military airfields.
Public water systems in Virginia are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
VA.attribution: Based on Virginia Department of Environmental Quality publications.

paragraphs: Washington state has an active PFAS program through the Department of Ecology and the State Department of Health. Ecology published a Chemical Action Plan for PFAS in 2020 that set state action levels for drinking water, though these were advisory rather than enforceable MCLs. The state health department later issued State Action Levels of 10 parts per trillion for PFOA, 15 parts per trillion for PFOS, 65 parts per trillion for PFHxS, 9 parts per trillion for PFNA, and 345 parts per trillion for PFBS.

The Washington legislature was the first in the country to ban PFAS in food packaging, through a 2018 law that took effect once safer alternatives were identified. Washington has also banned PFAS in firefighting foam and in cosmetics. State records show contamination has been identified near Joint Base Lewis-McChord and the former Naval Air Station Whidbey Island, as well as the Airway Heights area near Fairchild Air Force Base outside Spokane.

Public water systems in Washington are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029, in addition to the state action level framework.
WA.attribution: Based on Washington State Department of Ecology and Washington State Department of Health publications.

paragraphs: West Virginia is the origin state of the modern PFAS crisis. For decades the DuPont Washington Works plant in Parkersburg discharged PFOA, trade name C8, into the Ohio River and surrounding groundwater, contaminating drinking water for tens of thousands of residents in Parkersburg, Lubeck, Vienna, and across the river in Ohio. The C8 Health Project, funded by a DuPont settlement, enrolled over 69,000 residents and produced the epidemiological evidence linking PFOA to kidney cancer, testicular cancer, ulcerative colitis, thyroid disease, high cholesterol, and pregnancy-induced hypertension.

In 2024 the West Virginia legislature passed HB 3189, the Public Health Protection Act, which directed the Department of Environmental Protection to adopt state MCLs for six PFAS compounds. State records show the resulting rule broadly aligns with the federal EPA MCLs finalized in April 2024.

Litigation has been extensive. DuPont and its successor Chemours have paid hundreds of millions of dollars in settlements and judgments to West Virginia residents, medical monitoring recipients, and downstream utilities. Public water systems in West Virginia are subject to both the state MCLs and the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
WV.attribution: Based on West Virginia Department of Environmental Protection publications, C8 Health Project records, and federal court records.

paragraphs: Wisconsin adopted state drinking water standards for PFAS in 2022 through the Department of Natural Resources under NR 809. The Wisconsin standard sets a combined limit of 70 parts per trillion for the sum of PFOA and PFOS. This combined threshold is less strict than the federal EPA individual MCLs of 4 parts per trillion for each compound finalized in April 2024, and Wisconsin utilities now must meet the stricter federal standards.

The impetus for Wisconsin's regulatory response came from the Marinette and Peshtigo area, where the Tyco Fire Products facility used and disposed of AFFF firefighting foam for training exercises starting in the 1960s. PFOA and PFOS were detected in extraction wells at levels exceeding 10,000 parts per trillion, and Tyco and parent company Johnson Controls signed a settlement funding an alternate water supply program that still includes bottled water deliveries to some households. Other contaminated sites include the Madison area around Truax Field and various paper mill legacy sites in the Fox Valley.

The Wisconsin legislature passed Act 101 of 2021, which established a framework for state PFAS regulation and included some enforcement tools. Governor Tony Evers and Republican legislative leaders have had ongoing disputes over expanding PFAS response funding, and the state has appropriated funding for impacted well owners in several budget cycles.

Litigation has been active. The Wisconsin Attorney General has sued 3M, DuPont, Tyco, and other manufacturers. State records show the DNR maintains a PFAS Action Council and publishes a public dashboard tracking contamination sites. Public water systems in Wisconsin are subject to both the state NR 809 standard and the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
WI.attribution: Based on Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources publications, Wisconsin Attorney General filings, and Wisconsin legislative records.

paragraphs: As of April 2026, Wyoming has not adopted state-level PFAS drinking water standards beyond the federal EPA limits finalized in April 2024. The Wyoming Department of Environmental Quality has tracked contamination near Francis E. Warren Air Force Base outside Cheyenne.
Public water systems in Wyoming are subject to the federal compliance timeline requiring initial monitoring by 2027 and treatment installation by 2029.
WY.attribution: Based on Wyoming Department of Environmental Quality publications.
